item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It makes the line "We're back here. She's back." a whole lot more sinister.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The small retcon to Arcadia makes this more unnerving. +1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The two images used in this article are cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image was made by VWXYZ and the second image was taken by the author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe the real game, which has Leo in it instead of Joey, makes people smarter instead of dumber.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright, so here we have a learning video game that causes mental degradation through graphics shenanigans and general electric spookiness. Another skip achieving its effect through graphic wizardry would be Roget's own SCP-2600 which actually directly links back to 1070. Seems fitting that the connection should work both ways. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

However, if a subject over the age of fourteen uses SCP-1070, a different effect will occur. As the subject advances through SCP-1070's content, SCP-1070-B will begin asking more esoteric and incomprehensible questions, often using graphical errors and obfuscating graphics to prevent the subject from progressing. During this time, subjects will experience mental degradation. If SCP-1070 is played longer than 1 hour, the subject will have reverted to the mental capacity of a prepubescent.

To this:

However, if a subject over the age of fourteen uses SCP-1070, a different effect will occur. As the subject advances through SCP-1070's content, SCP-1070-B will begin asking more esoteric and incomprehensible questions, often using graphical errors and obfuscating graphics to prevent the subject from progressing. During this time, subjects will experience mental degradation. If SCP-1070 is played longer than 1 hour, the subject will have reverted to the mental capacity of a prepubescent.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find the fact that the game is called "Learning with Leo" but the person that teaches you is called Joey kinda unsettling, maybe it's a bootleg or maybe it's just a spooky coding ghost dude bro.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"+1, as normally video games do not cause mental retardation"

I don't know why I find this quote so fucking funny but I do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So Basically a video game which causes mental retardation. +1, as normally video games do not cause mental retardation, and the way it's presented is creepy as fuck.

Side note

Wouldn't that little green bugger being rendered like that on something like an NES be an anomaly in of itself?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now this, I would play. Nice work, you just made a philosopher's perfect video game :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would disagree and say that the specifics of how it works, and the personality of the object, elevate it above being a magic item. Did you read the entire article? The interview goes a long way towards providing the object's personality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: -1 It's a video game that makes you stupid. More of a thing that does a thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Creepy, just… creepy, I hate that green monster, I… oh god

upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed a 1070 that looked like it was supposed to be a 1070-B. Revert if I'm a derp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Both the old and the new version have great eerie qualities which I very appreciate about the SCP. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks bae

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just noting here that I replaced the image with a scaled-down version, because the original was 1.5Mb, and that's just evil. The original is still available in the Files section.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't be alarmed. You only have tomorrow to fear.

oh gods

Mom will be back. Everything will be A-Okay.

This thing has so little that I want so much more, yet more would risk ruining the article altogether

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's amazing that this SCP has received so much attention since I first posted it two years ago. Thanks so much to Roget for revising!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I see it, since it starts "messing with" users based on their age, it would still start the anomalous-behavior stuff, it would just conclude quickly since the goal brain-state is already present.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just out of curiosity, what happens if an adult with the mind of a prepubescent (like, say… me) were to play the game?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The effect is kinda eh, but some of the details are great: the game being from 10 years before the copyright, the title having nothing whatsoever to do with the content, and yes, the transcript, with Mom.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Better then it was, but leaving the downvote. Sorry, but it just feels like an early draft.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whats this? One of my old favorites re-written? Well, thats pretty awesome.

item.title: Really cleaned up this article
item.content:encoded:
I remember reading this when I wasn't a member yet and thinking that this was kind of eh and a little too creepypasta-ish. I came back, now being a member, to comment on it and perhaps downvote it.

I came back and found this rewrite, and it's fantastic now. +1.

(Also, first post wootwoot!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Deliberately ambiguous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just wondering, how old is the D-Class in the last bit? I'm assuming above 14 because of the anomalies, but it doesn't really state.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this reads much better now, although may I suggest that the subject's voice would raise in pitch after regressing, not lower?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still not crazy about the "mom" bit but ultimately it's better, enough for an upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This thing is just creepily incomprehensible enough to win me over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I, for one, do not think that the idea sounds cliche, and has a great deal of potential. I think that the rewrite has successfully repaired the article, and it deserves to exist above a 0 rating.

My suggestions for improving the hook and creepiness factors would have to be more examples of the conversations 1070-B conducts. Any instance where the reader sits and thinks "what the fuck does that mean? It's so weird." Is a good place for something creepy to wriggle its way into the mind.

Any sort of upgrade to the interview, or additional interviews, would probably improve the hook greatly. Examples I can think of would be any kind of description of the graphical anomalies (some people get -really- freaked out by this kind of thing), or perhaps any number of anomalous sounds. I suggest, like the static, any kind of spontaneous loud glitchy sound to break a silence or feed back into the subject.

I would also suggest any pictures of the glitches, or recordings of the audio glitches, but I understand that retrieving such things might not be so trivial.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Addendum seems to be trying a bit too hard, but the rest of it is good. Upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unfortunately, I still don't like it, and the downvote remains.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty nice. Nothing outstanding, but an educational game that turns you into a child and you can't use it to turn yourself back no matter how hard you try is a nice concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's definitely better, but overall I'm still not too crazy about this. It still seems like it's really.. cliche, I guess. I can't really put my finger on it, but if I had to guess, I'd say that it just seems like it's trying too hard to be spooky and mysterious. That isn't to say that's a fault of the rewrite, I just think it's the general vibe of the SCP concept itself.

Still, you improved it, so I've changed my downvote to a neutral vote.

item.title: REWRITE
item.content:encoded: This entry has been rewritten. Please take the time to re-evaluate it if you have not already done so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea is interesting to me in the same way Boggles is: it doesn't always mess with you, it chooses its victims and the methods for that victim. But there's nothing here that's particularly scary until you get to the log and see how it's "terrorizing" people.

I'd like to see more specifics on how it's terrorizing kids. Does it only pick ones with trauma, or maybe induces trauma in otherwise mentally stable kids? Or, like in the log, could its goal be to damage in the long term - hearing a game console whisper for a few months straight that your mom was murdered instead of dying of a heart attack?

Without something more to explain what's so creepy about it, it's too vague and informal on the tone for an upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to see a rewrite. It's a solid idea, and the picture creeps me the hell out, but the text is just too "and then a skeleton popped out" for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's an okay SCP, and a nice source of creepypasta.

Upvote.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Try mailing the author. If you get no response, I'll tentatively give you permission. You have until Friday, January 4, 2013 to get a draft ready. No deletion vote will be started until that time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has multiple tone issues, and reads like a bad creepypasta. I would like to see a rewrite, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dang, I've been waffling about the past couple of days trying to decide if I wanted to ask to do a rewrite here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to try rewriting this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted at the very first sentence: "Personnel must keep frequent watch over SCP-1070."

Er, isn't that what the whole Foundation is for?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Say what you like about the tenets of National Socialism, Dude, at least it's an ethos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted in the hopes of this one being saved… Personnally, I like the Existential Philosophy bit and would love to see this thing do just that, but I think that would also make for "Thing what crazies you to death," except this is one that logics you to death. This might be beyond saving though, and that's a little sad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Existential philosophy

Careful, dude, they're nihilists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At the moment, this feels too much like those really gross, overdone creepypastas about 'haunted video game cartridges' where all the story is composed of is gore and scary pictures and screaming and OH NO BLOOD ON THE CARPET and AND THEN MARIO KILLED HIMSELF.
However, there are many times where messed-up video games/television shows/etc have worked, and this SCP can do the same, with some revision. I recommend cutting out the 'WHOA SCARY PICTURES' and 'EXISTENTIAL PHILOSOPHY' and pay more attention to what was really freaky to us as kids (don't use gore and loud noises as your one and only source of horror).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At it's core, I love this idea. I mean, look at that picture! I'm a sucker for glitchy horrors and this thing would be right up my alley if it was a bit more subtle.

But that's the thing about it: it's not subtle at all. It doesn't just try to freak children out by being weird and unnerving, it tries to do shock horror with gore and blood and screaming. And when it's not trying to shock you, it's either being vague (mind games?) or being outright silly (a five year olds worst nightmare: EXISTENTIAL PHILOSOPHY). On top of all that, the tone is downright awful. "not larger than a shoebox", "turning on inexplicably", these are just some of the fragments that take me out of the experience, and that's not something that should happen.

If you want to write something scary that draws upon fear of children, that's fine. God knows it worked for Candle Cove. But if you're going to do that, you have to take a step back and look at it through the eyes of a child. Some of the best things that scared us when we were kids didn't try to shock us with gruesome imagery or really really loud noises.

For instance, a lot of people remember being scared of King Ramses from the Courage the Cowardly Dog episode "King Ramses' Curse". Looking at it as an adult, you know it's just some early 2000s CGI and it makes you laugh, but as a kid you're not thinking about the technology behind it, and often times you're not really listening to what its saying either. All you're thinking is "This thing is moving wrong and it's creepy looking and its voice is echoing too much and MAKE IT GO AWAY I DON'T LIKE IT."

Trying to take things that scare adults and turn it on children often doesn't work as intended, as this article shows. I mean, what about if the video game happened to glitch out of its own accord, and when a child sees this glitchy mess and sees images like the page image, it scares them. But the anomalous bit is that the fear never goes away, and as they grow up the fear just intensifies to the point that when they're an adult they can't even think to play the game because they're too afraid of what happens when it glitches out. Like a reverse effect of what usually happens with childhood fear.

That's just off the top of my head, and I know this isn't the time or place to start pitching ideas. However, I want to see this article done right because like I said I love stories of this nature. As it stands, however, I don't like it and I'm giving it a -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted previously, but on second glance… Ehhh, it's just okay. Gaffney's got some good points, as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reads like a mediocre creppypasta, both in terms of effect and tone. Seriously, there is a format for SCP articles; it's not as though we're still on /x/ and are just making up terms (TS-,OP-). Minus one.

item.title: Re: Staf Post
item.content:encoded:
all hail staf

In all seriousness, my thoughts haven't changed since this was posted. Definitely not good, but borders on being just not bad enough that I didn't downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fairly uninteresting. -1 Looks like I've already downvoted this. Huh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is this Roget's first action as staff? Yay!

Anyway moving on to the actual scip. I got a sinking suspicion I wouldn't like this as soon as I read "The current container for SCP-1070 is a console that seems like a video game system.", and loe and behold I was right. The writing is pretty mediocre, bordering on bad, and the effect was pretty bland. It reminds me too much of Bobble.

Also

SCP-1070 itself presents users with terrorizing information; topics include existential philosophy

I actually laughed out loud at this part. Really author? Really?

Also also

Because the age group targeted by SCP-1070 is between 3 and 10 years old, users may experience strong anxiety attacks, entertain suicidal thoughts, or become catatonic.

I can tell you from experience that seeing violent shit did not make me consider suicide as a kid. Plus, this is kind of stupid. It doesn't say anything about it /only/ being visible to kids, so that would imply that the Foundation is showing this to kids for… what reason? Shit n giggles?

"Hey Bob I'm pretty bored. Wanna go show some kids these secret anomalous artifacts?"
"I see no problem with that course of action Alice."

Because if they're not, the users would just be adults, probably D-Class, who don't give no fuck about seeing violence and wouldn't start thinking about suicide because of it. Maybe I'm just being obtuse though.

-1

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
I'm putting this article up for review. Please take the time to look at it, vote, and comment if you haven't already done so.

I apparently thought this was some hot shit back in the day, but re-reading it now… yeah, no. The article feels like a creepypasta crushed into the SCP format. There are numerous tonal errors, and I'm confused as to what this object is even supposed to look like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: <Insert witty thing here to excuse my not understanding basic grammar>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
your thinking

In anycase, they both are meant to hide non-essential information. However, the reason that they can be scary is the implication behind why they couldnt divulge that info while they could freely divulge a plethora of other knowledge behind the object. As the 835 example has already been used, I'll use a different one. I may be incorrect for thinking this, but you could almost consider SCP-055 a constant self-expungement. We dont know a lot about it. All we know is that we can't remember it, it was apparently dangerous and hard to contain that we assigned it Keter (either because of or despite the fact that we can't remember what exactly we're guarding), and that it's not a sphere. That's it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No need to become hostile or directly insulting people. And actually, [REDACTED] and [DATA EXPUNGED] are used in *both* instances. They could be used to redact the names of places because honestly they're not necessary; they could also be used to not give out *too* much detail, and let the mind create its own horror.

That last one is tricky: it took me quite a bit to get it right myself. You can't just say "If a person sees this object, then [DATA EXPUNGED]" or "The subject [REDACTED] and then fell over" because there's no real hook. My favorite generic example is something more along the lines of "If exposed, primary symptoms last for approximately thirty minutes before [DATA REDACTED] requiring level 3 biological contamination quarantine."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you're thinking of black boxes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-835
That's how you use Redacted.

The "Scary bits go here" were redacted, then elaborated upon.
Without the elaboration and the whole "clearance thing" 835 wouldn't be as popular as it is.

But you are right, it's just not my personal taste.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…Yes, because that's actually what it's supposed to do. That's the entire point of redactions, to hide salient pieces of information and make you come up with the interesting bits for yourself.

*snip*. -Moose.
